name: Shikoku
history: Shikoku, Japan's smallest main island, has been inhabited since prehistoric times and developed as an agricultural center during the feudal period under various daimyo rulers. The island was historically divided into four provinces, which is reflected in its name meaning 'four countries.' During the Edo period, Shikoku remained relatively isolated from central Japanese politics, allowing its local culture and traditions to flourish independently. The modern era brought industrial development and improved transportation links, though the island has maintained much of its traditional character compared to more urbanized regions.
culturalNotes: Shikoku is most famous as the home of the Shikoku Pilgrimage, a 1,200-kilometer sacred Buddhist circuit visiting 88 temples that has attracted pilgrims for centuries and remains a deeply spiritual journey. The island is known for its distinctive Awa Odori dance festival in Tokushima, featuring energetic group performances that draw thousands of visitors annually. Traditional crafts such as indigo dyeing and pottery production continue to thrive in local communities, reflecting Shikoku's strong connection to artisanal heritage and rural lifestyles.
bestTimeToVisit: October through November provides pleasant temperatures and clear skies.
languageNotes: The Shikoku dialect, particularly the Awa dialect of Tokushima, preserves older Japanese linguistic features and can be quite distinct from standard Japanese. Useful phrases include 'Awa Odori e iku' (going to see the Awa Odori) and 'henro michi' (pilgrimage trail), terms deeply embedded in local culture and conversation.

places.name: Kochi
places.description: The largest city in Shikoku, known for its historic castle, lively Sunday market, and gateway to the island's cultural attractions. The castle is one of Japan's finest remaining feudal structures and offers sweeping views of the city.

activities.detail: Explore one of Japan's finest remaining feudal castles, built in 1603, featuring original wooden structures and panoramic city views from the keep.
Browse the lively open-air market that has operated every Sunday for nearly 300 years, selling local produce, crafts, and street food along Otsuji Street.
Take a scenic boat ride or kayak trip on the last clear river in Japan, known for its pristine waters and dramatic gorge scenery.
Hike or drive to this hilltop park for 360-degree views of Kochi City, the Seto Inland Sea, and surrounding mountains, especially beautiful at sunset.
Learn about regional history and art, including samurai artifacts, traditional crafts, and exhibits on the Tosa domain's cultural heritage.
Stroll this historic covered shopping arcade filled with traditional shops, local boutiques, and casual eateries in the heart of the city.

food.detail: Seared bonito (skipjack tuna) served rare with garlic, ginger, and soy sauce—a Kochi specialty that showcases the region's fresh seafood.
Grilled skewers of local Tosa free-range chicken, prized for its flavorful meat and traditionally enjoyed with local sake.
A warming noodle soup unique to Kochi, combining wheat noodles with fish broth, vegetables, and local ingredients in a savory broth.
Sample Tosa sake at local breweries and bars, as Kochi Prefecture is renowned for high-quality sake production dating back centuries.
Local ramen featuring rich tonkotsu or seafood-based broths, often topped with tender pork and regional vegetables.

culture.detail: At the Sunday Market, arrive early for the best selection and engage politely with vendors; photography is generally welcome but always ask permission first.
When visiting nearby temples and shrines, wear respectful clothing; remove shoes when entering sacred spaces and bow upon entry and exit.
Kochi residents are passionate about their local sake and cuisine—showing genuine interest in regional products and traditions is greatly appreciated.
Plan visits around Yosakoi Matsuri (August) if possible, when the city celebrates with energetic traditional dance competitions that define local cultural identity.

places.name: Takamatsu
places.description: The capital of Kagawa Prefecture and northern Shikoku's main hub, famous for its beautiful Ritsurin Garden and as the gateway to Naoshima and the Seto Inland Sea islands. The city blends traditional charm with modern amenities.

activities.detail: One of Japan's most celebrated landscape gardens featuring a scenic pond, traditional teahouse, and carefully pruned pine trees that showcase Edo-period garden design.
Historic castle grounds with reconstructed gates and moats overlooking Takamatsu Harbor, offering insight into the city's samurai past and modern cherry blossom viewing.
Day trip via 50-minute ferry to Japan's renowned contemporary art island, home to the Benesse Art Museum and outdoor installations.
Museum showcasing local history, archaeology, and art with rotating exhibitions covering samurai heritage and modern Kagawa culture.
Historic Buddhist temple perched on a plateau with sweeping views of the Seto Inland Sea and traditional pilgrimage significance.
Modern harbor development area with shops, restaurants, and evening illuminations perfect for evening strolls and contemporary city atmosphere.

food.detail: Kagawa's signature thick wheat noodles served in a light dashi broth, famously eaten at casual standing shops throughout the city.
Local brown sugar soft serve ice cream made from Kagawa's distinctive black sugar, a popular street snack with subtle caramel notes.

food.text: Oden at Takamatsu Chuo Shopping Street
food.detail: Winter comfort food featuring stewed vegetables and fish cakes available at small shops in the covered arcade near Takamatsu Station.
Seafood and vegetable rolled sushi featuring local Seto Inland Sea ingredients, often found at department store food halls and restaurants.
Sweet soy sauce-glazed dumplings that are a traditional Takamatsu souvenir and snack, served warm at street vendors near temples.

culture.detail: Mid-August festival featuring Awa Odori folk dance performances throughout the city—visiting during this time offers vibrant parades and communal celebration.
Takamatsu's revered gardens like Ritsurin expect visitors to move quietly and respectfully, reflecting Zen principles central to Japanese garden appreciation.
Local restaurants and udon stands often have standing-only seating and quick service culture—expect efficiency rather than lingering, and appreciate the straightforward hospitality.
Takamatsu residents frequently visit Seto Inland Sea islands by ferry as a weekend activity—ferries run regularly and island-hopping is a normalized local lifestyle choice.

places.name: Naoshima
places.description: A world-renowned contemporary art island in the Seto Inland Sea hosting the Benesse Art Museum, outdoor installations, and artist residencies. It's one of Japan's most important modern art destinations and attracts design-conscious travelers globally.

activities.detail: A world-class contemporary art institution built into a hillside overlooking the Seto Inland Sea, featuring rotating exhibitions and a permanent collection of modern and contemporary works.
An underground museum designed by Tadao Ando that houses monumental works by Monet, James Turrell, and Walter De Maria in a minimalist concrete structure.
Yayoi Kusama's iconic yellow polka-dotted pumpkin installation sits at the ferry terminal, creating a whimsical entry point to the island and a famous photo spot.
Artist residency spaces and galleries showcasing work by contemporary and emerging artists, often with site-specific installations reflecting the island's unique landscape.
A smaller venue featuring experimental and provocative contemporary work, often hosting artist talks and community-oriented programs.
A scenic 16-kilometer loop around Naoshima by bicycle offers coastal views, hidden shrines, and access to remote art installations away from main museums.
The historic village area features narrow lanes, traditional Edo-period houses, and contemporary art projects integrated into the vernacular architecture.

food.detail: The Seto Inland Sea provides exceptional seasonal seafood, and island restaurants serve ultra-fresh sashimi and grilled fish caught daily from local waters.
The island produces a distinctive artisanal soy sauce using traditional fermentation methods passed down for generations, available at local shops and restaurants.
Naoshima's warm climate yields sweet mandarin oranges, lemons, and seasonal vegetables that appear in farm-to-table dishes at island restaurants.
Contemporary cafés near museums offer carefully plated set lunches featuring local ingredients, often with artistic presentation that complements the island's creative atmosphere.
Casual dining spots serve savory pancakes and udon noodles prepared with local seafood and vegetables, offering affordable sustenance for art-viewing days.

culture.detail: Naoshima has limited lodging options and fills quickly during peak seasons and art events, requiring reservations months ahead rather than walk-in visits.
Many galleries and museums on the island prohibit photography inside exhibition spaces, and violations can result in visitor bans; always check signage before photographing.

culture.text: Allow Full Days for Museum Visits
culture.detail: The Benesse and Chichu museums are intentionally designed for contemplative, slow viewing rather than quick tours, and rushing diminishes the intended artistic experience.
The island maintains a peaceful atmosphere with expectations of low-volume conversation and mindful behavior; loud groups and commercial photography are discouraged by the community.

places.name: Matsuyama
places.description: Ehime's cultural capital, home to Matsuyama Castle and Dogo Onsen, Japan's oldest continuously operating hot spring resort featured in literary classics. The city blends castle heritage with traditional bathing culture and charming old town neighborhoods.

activities.detail: A hilltop samurai fortress with twelve original structures, offering panoramic city views and accessible by ropeway or chairlift from the base station.
Japan's oldest continuously operating hot spring resort, famous for its historic bathhouse architecture and featured prominently in Soseki Natsume's literary work.
The iconic three-story wooden bathhouse built in 1894 with its distinctive tower, offering traditional bathing rituals and a nostalgic Meiji-era atmosphere.
A beautifully reconstructed samurai garden adjacent to Matsuyama Castle featuring seasonal flowers, tea houses, and traditional Japanese landscape design.
An ancient Shinto shrine with a thousand-year history located near Dogo, known for its peaceful forest setting and traditional architecture.
A preserved neighborhood with traditional wooden merchant houses, cafes, and shops reflecting Edo and Meiji-period streetscapes.
A contemporary art museum showcasing both Japanese and international works in a striking modern building with rotating exhibitions.

food.detail: A local soy-based ramen with thin noodles in a light, delicate broth often topped with pork and green onions, distinctly lighter than tonkotsu varieties.
A rustic hot pot dish made with taro root, chicken, and seasonal vegetables simmered in a savory broth, traditionally eaten in autumn and winter.
Fresh local sea bream served over rice with dashi broth poured over it, highlighting Matsuyama's access to the Seto Inland Sea.
Silky soft tofu specialties served at traditional restaurants near Dogo Onsen, often enjoyed as kaiseki or simple preparations showcasing the ingredient.
Ehime's famous citrus feature in everything from fresh fruit to juice, mikan-flavored sweets, and mikan ice cream throughout the city.

culture.detail: Bathe naked in gender-separated areas, wash thoroughly before entering the communal bath, and keep towels out of the water—this respectful practice is essential.
Matsuyama deeply embraces its connection to Soseki Natsume's novel Botchan; visitors often seek out settings from the book and reflect on the city's role in Japanese literature.
Matsuyama hosts traditional matsuri throughout the year, including the Matsuyama Autumn Festival in October featuring parades and portable shrines—plan visits around these celebrations.
When visiting Isaniwa Shrine and other sacred sites, bow before crossing torii gates, rinse hands and mouth at purification basins, and move quietly through the grounds.

places.name: Dogo Onsen
places.description: A historic hot spring resort village dating back 3,000 years with a beautifully preserved traditional bathhouse and charming ryokan-lined streets. The atmospheric setting inspired Spirited Away and offers both cultural immersion and relaxation.

activities.detail: Bathe in the iconic three-story wooden bathhouse built in 1894, featuring the famous Yushinden bath on the top floor with views of the surrounding town.
Explore the atmospheric lantern-lit streets and traditional machiya buildings that inspired Hayao Miyazaki's acclaimed film, particularly around the main bathhouse complex.
Watch the automated wooden puppet show featuring characters from Natsume Soseki's novel 'Botchan' perform every hour outside the bathhouse.
Walk through the hillside park overlooking the town and explore the historical remains of Matsuyama Castle perched above.
Visit smaller public bathhouses like Asuka-no-Yu and Yuno-no-Yu scattered throughout the village, each with their own mineral composition and local character.

activities.text: Traditional ryokan stay with kaiseki dinner
activities.detail: Spend an evening in a family-run inn enjoying multi-course Matsuyama cuisine and onsen bathing in private settings.

food.detail: Local specialty featuring tender sea bream cooked with rice, dashi, and seasonal vegetables, traditionally served in wooden boxes at Dogo restaurants.
Hearty regional comfort dish made with taro potatoes, chicken, and local ingredients, often enjoyed as a warming meal after bathing.
Locally-made pan-fried dumplings with distinctive thin skin and savory pork-vegetable filling, sold at dedicated shops throughout the onsen village.
Three-colored sweet dumplings inspired by the classic novel, combining red bean, egg custard, and matcha flavors, sold as a signature souvenir.
Golden fried fish cake made with local white fish and seasonings, enjoyed as a street snack or appetizer in the onsen district.

culture.detail: Always rinse thoroughly before entering the communal bath, and keep your small towel out of the water as a sign of respect for fellow bathers.

culture.text: Timing your visit for fewer crowds
culture.detail: Visit early mornings or weekday afternoons to experience the onsen's peaceful atmosphere, as evenings and weekends draw significant tourist crowds.
If staying in a ryokan, wear your yukata with the left side over the right (right over left is reserved for deceased), and wear provided slippers outside your room.
Different bathhouses display water temperature in kanji or colors; 'atsui' (hot) is typically 42-44°C, while 'ぬるい' (lukewarm) is gentler for longer soaking.

places.name: Iya Gorge
places.description: A dramatically deep mountain gorge in central Shikoku featuring sheer cliffs, emerald-green river waters, and traditional vine bridges crossed by few visitors. The remote landscape epitomizes Shikoku's wild, unspoiled natural beauty.

activities.detail: Walk across the reconstructed traditional vine bridge spanning the gorge, one of Japan's three most famous vine bridges, requiring sure footing and a head for heights.
Take a guided wooden boat through the narrowest, most dramatic section of the gorge where 500-meter cliffs rise vertically from emerald waters.
Trek through pristine mountain trails connecting remote villages, with routes ranging from easy riverside walks to challenging ridge hikes offering panoramic views.
Cross the dramatic 45-meter-high steel suspension bridge offering vertiginous views down to the Yoshii River and surrounding forest canopy.
Explore this historic temple perched on a cliff face, accessible by a steep rope ladder and offering serene mountain vistas from its precarious location.
Soak in traditional hot springs fed by mountain waters, available at small ryokans and public bathhouses throughout the valley villages.
Visit during spring and early summer when mountain azaleas, rhododendrons, and wildflowers blanket the gorge slopes in vibrant colors.

food.detail: Fresh grilled sweetfish caught from the Yoshii River, seasoned simply with salt and served whole as a delicate local specialty.
Handmade buckwheat noodles prepared at small mountain restaurants using pure mountain spring water and often served in rustic wooden settings.
Translucent devil's tongue jelly blocks served chilled as a refreshing side dish or in hot pots, made using traditional methods in the valley.
Regional yuzu juice, yuzu miso, and yuzu-flavored dishes showcasing the tart citrus fruit that thrives in the mountain climate.
Venison and wild boar stews and grilled preparations reflecting the rural hunting traditions of mountain Shikoku communities.

culture.detail: Iya Gorge is extremely remote with limited accommodations, restaurants, and services—plan ahead, bring supplies, and check weather and road conditions carefully.
These bridges, though reconstructed for safety, are sacred sites with deep historical and spiritual significance; walk respectfully and avoid loud behavior.
The gorge was historically a refuge for defeated samurai and political exiles; many villages maintain this heritage and appreciate respectful interest in local stories.
Weather changes rapidly in the gorge with sudden fog and rain; wear layers, sturdy hiking boots, and waterproof gear even on clear days.

places.name: Cape Muroto
places.description: A rugged coastal headland on Kochi's southeastern tip with dramatic sea cliffs, a historic lighthouse, and significant Buddhist pilgrimage heritage. The wild seascape and isolation make it one of Japan's most striking coastal destinations.

activities.detail: Climb the historic 1889 lighthouse for panoramic views of the Pacific Ocean and dramatic coastal cliffs, one of Japan's oldest lighthouses still in operation.
Walk the scenic clifftop trails with observation decks overlooking turquoise waters, sea stacks, and the distinctive Muroto headland geology.
Visit the 24th temple on the Shikoku Pilgrimage route, perched dramatically on the cape's edge with stunning ocean vistas and historical Buddhist significance.
Explore guided geological tours explaining the Muroto Peninsula's 100-million-year-old rock formations and tectonic uplift visible in the coastal cliffs.
Hike the rugged 90-minute clifftop walking path connecting Muroto cape with dramatic seascapes, wildflowers, and access to hidden coves.
Learn about the cape's geological history, maritime heritage, and pilgrimage traditions through exhibits housed in a modern facility near the lighthouse.

food.detail: Seared bonito, a local specialty in Kochi, is briefly flame-grilled and served with ponzu, often sourced fresh from the rich Pacific waters near Muroto.
Muroto's rocky shoreline yields fresh sea urchin, typically enjoyed as sashimi or in rice bowls during spring and early summer.
Large communal platters of sashimi and local seafood arranged on wooden boards, a traditional Kochi dining style perfect for experiencing Muroto's catch.
Fresh-caught wild sea bass from the cape's rich fishing grounds, prepared simply as sashimi or grilled to highlight its delicate flavor.
Freshly caught local fish fried in crispy batter, a casual seaside specialty sold at small restaurants and stands near the cape.

culture.detail: If visiting Hotsumisaki Temple, observe respectful shrine customs including removing shoes in certain areas and purchasing pilgrim stamps if participating in the Shikoku route.
The cape is exposed to strong Pacific winds and sudden weather changes year-round; bring windproof layers and check forecasts, as conditions can shift rapidly.
Muroto is remote with limited shops and restaurants; stock supplies in town before heading to the cape, and note that many facilities have reduced hours outside peak season.
The area is a protected Japanese Geopark; stay on marked trails, do not remove rocks or specimens, and follow signage to preserve geological features for future visitors.

places.name: Oboke and Koboke Gorges
places.description: Twin mountain gorges straddling the Yoshii River known for boat tours through narrow canyons with striking rock formations and seasonal foliage. The scenic boat journey is one of Shikoku's most memorable experiences.

activities.detail: Navigate the narrow canyon passages of both gorges by traditional wooden boat, passing through dramatic rock formations and seasonal scenery.
Trek along riverside trails with panoramic views of the steep gorge walls and the Yoshii River below.
Visit the scenic overlook platform for photographs and sweeping vistas across the canyon landscape.
Experience vibrant autumn maple leaves or spring cherry blossoms reflecting in the river and framing the rock faces.
Navigate gentler stretches of the river in small groups for a more active water experience than the traditional boat tours.
Learn about the geological formation and local history of the gorges at the small visitor center near the boat dock.

food.detail: Fresh river ayu caught from the Yoshii River, grilled whole and salted, a regional specialty celebrating the local waters.

food.text: Imo Taki (Potato and Vegetable Hot Pot)
food.detail: A hearty mountain dish of layered potatoes, vegetables, and sometimes chicken simmered in a communal pot, warming and filling.
Dishes incorporating sudachi, a citrus fruit native to Shikoku, drizzled over grilled fish and vegetables for bright acidity.
Buckwheat noodles made with pristine water from the surrounding mountains, often served cold or in hot broth at riverside restaurants.
Local mochi-like sweets sold at stalls near the boat docks, often flavored with local ingredients or wrapped in leaves.

culture.detail: Wear non-slip shoes and hold railings securely; boatmen may navigate shallow or turbulent sections where stability matters for safety and respect.
Arrive early to secure boat tour spots, especially during autumn foliage season when tours fill quickly and morning light illuminates the gorge walls best.
Heavy rainfall can sometimes close boat tours temporarily; check conditions ahead, and note that spring and autumn offer the most reliable scheduling.
The gorges hold spiritual significance in Japanese nature reverence; stay on marked paths, carry out all trash, and avoid loud behavior to preserve the tranquil atmosphere.

places.name: Shimanto River
places.description: Japan's longest river system that flows through Kochi's rural heartland, famous for its pristine waters, traditional fishing villages, and scenic cycling routes. The low bridges crossing the river become submerged during seasonal floods, creating unique photogenic moments.

activities.detail: Pedal along dedicated cycling routes that wind through rural villages and riverside valleys, with rental shops available in Shimanto town.
Watch traditional nighttime cormorant fishing from May to October, where trained birds dive to catch ayu (sweetfish) in the illuminated river.
Visit during rainy seasons (June, September) to photograph the iconic low bridges vanishing beneath floodwaters, particularly around Shimanto town.
Participate in seasonal ayu fishing using traditional techniques or wade in the pristine waters to catch sweetfish yourself during summer months.
Hike to this hilltop viewpoint overlooking the meandering Shimanto River and surrounding forested valleys for panoramic photography.
Explore this preserved traditional settlement with old wooden houses and narrow lanes that showcase Edo-period riverside village architecture.
Paddle a kayak through calm sections of the Shimanto to experience its clear waters and spot wildlife including herons and kingfishers.

food.detail: Grilled or simmered whole ayu caught fresh from the river is a seasonal delicacy, available spring through fall at riverside restaurants.
Lightly seared bonito caught in nearby waters is served as tataki-style (surface-seared) with citrus and soy, a Kochi specialty.
Freshly grilled skewered dumplings sold by street vendors along the cycling routes, typically served with sweet or savory sauce.
Spring bamboo shoots foraged from local mountains are pickled and pressed into sushi rolls by traditional riverside restaurants.
The river's pristine mineral water is bottled and used locally to brew tea and prepare traditional dishes, prized for its purity.

culture.detail: Plan visits during June and September rainy seasons for submerged bridge photos, but avoid crossing low bridges during active flooding for safety.
Nod and speak quietly when encountering traditional cormorant fishermen or ayu fishermen at dawn, as disturbing their work is considered disrespectful.
Buy local products and eat at small family-run restaurants rather than chain stores, as tourism sustains these aging villages economically.
The river and villages operate on fishing and agricultural seasons—plan activities around ayu season (May-October) and avoid visiting during winter when many businesses close.

places.name: Yodo Island
places.description: A scenic island in the Seto Inland Sea easily accessible via the Seto Ohashi Bridge, featuring beautiful gardens, walking trails, and views across the archipelago. It serves as an ideal day trip or stop between Takamatsu and Okayama.

activities.detail: Explore the remnants of this historic castle perched on the island's hilltop, offering panoramic views of the Seto Inland Sea and surrounding islands.
Stroll through this traditional Japanese garden designed with seasonal plantings and stone arrangements that frame views of the inland sea.
Visit the dedicated observation area on the island to admire the engineering marvel of the bridge that connects Shikoku to Honshu.
Take a scenic 2-3 hour walking trail around the island's perimeter with stops at small beaches and fishing villages.
Learn about the island's history, the construction of the Seto Ohashi Bridge, and local maritime heritage through exhibits and photographs.
Visit this small seaside shrine at dawn to experience traditional island spirituality and catch golden light over the Seto Inland Sea.

food.detail: Fresh local sea bream prepared as sashimi or grilled, showcasing the clean, delicate flavor of fish from these waters.
The famous thick noodles from nearby Kagawa Prefecture, served at small island restaurants with rich dashi broth and local toppings.
A beloved summer refreshment sold at small stalls near the bridge approach, often flavored with local yuzu or plum syrup.
Dried seaweed harvested from island waters, sold as crispy snacks or accompaniments to rice at local shops.
Simple but satisfying bowl of rice topped with seasoned local nori and a raw egg, popular with day-trippers.

culture.text: Visit early morning or late afternoon
culture.detail: The island is most peaceful before midday crowds arrive from the bridge, allowing better appreciation of gardens and coastal views.

culture.text: Respect shrine customs at small roadside altars
culture.detail: You will encounter small shrines and offerings throughout the island; a brief bow when passing shows respect to local traditions.
Most attractions require traversing uneven stone paths, steep hills, and coastal trails without extensive paved infrastructure.
Many small food stalls and souvenir shops on Yodo Island do not accept credit cards, so carrying yen is essential.

places.name: Onomichi
places.description: A historic port town on the Seto Inland Sea known for its atmospheric narrow lanes, hillside temples, and as a cycling hub for the Shimanami Kaido route. Its nostalgic townscape and literary heritage make it a beloved filmmaker and artist destination.

activities.detail: Climb the atmospheric stone steps and narrow lanes through the historic hillside district, passing small shrines and traditional wooden buildings.
Rent a bicycle and ride across a series of bridges connecting Onomichi to nearby islands, one of Japan's most scenic cycling routes.
Visit this hillside Buddhist temple with views over the Seto Inland Sea and the town, accessible by ropeway or hiking trail.
Explore contemporary and traditional art installations in a modern museum overlooking the harbor and sea.
Walk along the waterfront promenade lined with sculptures and memorials celebrating the town's connection to Japanese literature and cinema.
Stroll through narrow lanes between the train station and harbor, discovering vintage shops, temples, and merchant houses from the Edo and Meiji periods.

food.detail: Local ramen topped with a rich pork and fish-based broth and flavored with soy sauce, often served with a raw egg and pork fatback.
Crispy octopus balls sold by street vendors throughout the harbor area, a beloved local snack with a molten center.
Charcoal-grilled chicken and vegetable skewers sold at small food stalls, seasoned with local sauce or salt.
Dine on seasonal catches including sea bream, squid, and white fish at waterfront restaurants and izakayas, often grilled or raw as sashimi.
Sample local lemon-infused desserts, drinks, and preserved goods from Onomichi's famous lemon cultivation heritage.

culture.detail: Many hillside temples close by early evening; plan temple visits before dusk and be mindful of quiet, meditative spaces.
Onomichi's charm lies in its steep, uneven stone lanes and stairs; wear comfortable, slip-resistant footwear to navigate safely.
The town rewards leisurely wandering rather than checking off attractions; allow time to get lost in narrow alleys and chat with shop owners.
Onomichi has been featured in multiple Japanese films and literary works; locals take pride in this artistic heritage, so showing respect for the cultural atmosphere is appreciated.

places.name: Shimanami Kaido
places.description: A 70-kilometer cycling route connecting Honshu and Shikoku across scenic islands with minimal traffic and stunning Seto Inland Sea views. It's become one of Japan's most famous and rewarding bicycle tours for both casual and serious cyclists.

activities.text: Cycle the full Shimanami Kaido route
activities.detail: Ride all 70 kilometers across ten bridges connecting Honshu to Shikoku, passing through Omishima, Ikuchijima, Innoshima, and Mukaijima islands.
Stop at this scenic viewpoint on Omishima island to watch the sunset over the Seto Inland Sea and rest between cycling segments.
Visit the contemporary art museums scattered across Ikuchijima, including installations in old buildings that blend modern art with island heritage.
Visit one of the many family-run mandarin orange and lemon farms on the islands to pick fruit seasonally or learn about local cultivation methods.
Pay respects at this historic Shinto shrine perched on Innoshima island, accessible by a short walk with views of the inland sea.

activities.text: Rent a bike from local shops
activities.detail: Use one of the well-marked rental stations at the Onomichi or Imabari terminals offering regular bicycles, e-bikes, and tandem options.

activities.text: Kayak or paddleboard in calm waters
activities.detail: Several islands offer water sports rentals to explore sheltered bays and coves around the route at a slower pace.

food.detail: Sample the region's famous fresh lemons, used in local desserts, drinks, and as a preserved condiment sold at shops throughout the islands.
Enjoy tender fried local octopus caught from the Seto Inland Sea, often served at small restaurants near the cycling route.
Try this light, soy-based local ramen style from Onomichi at the starting point, known for a broth featuring small sardines and lard.
Dine on fresh-caught sea bream, sea urchin, and other sashimi-grade fish grilled over charcoal at waterfront restaurants on Omishima and Innoshima.
Stop at roadside shops for creamy soft-serve ice cream flavored with local lemon, mandarin orange, or yuzu.

culture.text: Start early to avoid heat and crowds
culture.detail: Begin cycling in the early morning, especially in summer, as afternoons become hot and the route attracts tourist groups by midday.
The route is designed with separated cycling paths; stay in designated lanes and follow traffic signals to maintain safety and local harmony.
Stop at local cafes, farms, and family-run restaurants rather than chain convenience stores to sustain island communities that maintain the route.

culture.text: Observe quiet hours on residential island roads
culture.detail: Keep noise levels low when passing through village areas, especially early morning and evening, as residents live closely along the cycling paths.

places.name: Shikoku Pilgrimage Trail
places.description: A sacred 1,200-kilometer Buddhist pilgrimage circuit connecting 88 temples across all four prefectures, traditionally walked by millions seeking spiritual renewal. Walking the entire trail takes 30-60 days and offers profound cultural and spiritual immersion.

activities.detail: Begin or end your pilgrimage at this founding temple in Naruto where pilgrims traditionally receive their walking credentials and spiritual blessing.
Conclude the full circuit at this mountain temple in Kagawa where pilgrims celebrate finishing the 1,200-kilometer journey with a formal completion ceremony.
Trek the scenic coastal stretch between temples in Kochi prefecture where the trail follows pristine shorelines and offers ocean views.
Experience the challenging high-elevation sections between temples 19-23 in the Iya Valley with steep terrain and panoramic valley views.
Visit Zentsūji temple's museum to learn about the legendary founder Kōbō Daishi and the historical significance of the 88-temple circuit.
Stay at traditional pilgrim lodges (henro houses) that provide simple meals and shelter while connecting with fellow pilgrims and locals.

food.detail: Kagawa's famous thick wheat noodles served in light broth, traditionally eaten by pilgrims for energy and available at shops throughout Shikoku.
Katsuo no tataki is lightly seared bonito fish sliced thin and served with soy-based dipping sauce, a Kochi specialty reflecting the region's fishing heritage.
A vegetable-based soup with tofu and root vegetables traditionally prepared at temples and henro houses to sustain pilgrims during their journey.
Sweet skewered dumplings glazed with soy sauce sold at temple gift shops and roadside stalls as quick pilgrim snacks.
Kochi's signature yuzu fruit appears in teas, preserves, and local dishes, offering refreshing sustenance for walkers on hot sections of the trail.

culture.detail: Purchase a henro-nokori (pilgrimage booklet) at the starting temple to collect unique stamps at each of the 88 temples, creating an official record of your spiritual progress.
Dress in traditional white henro garments including a vest and hat that identify you as a pilgrim and demonstrate respect for the sacred journey to locals and other walkers.
Follow protocols such as removing shoes before entering temple buildings, making offerings at main halls, and respectfully greeting priests who may offer guidance or blessings.
Engage with other henro (pilgrims) and locals who often offer hospitality, directions, and meals as acts of spiritual merit called 'osettai'—a cherished custom of supporting fellow walkers.

places.name: Uwajima
places.description: A coastal town in southern Ehime famous for its seafood, traditional castle, and cultural festivals featuring rare bullfighting traditions. The town offers authentic rural Shikoku charm and excellent local cuisine.

activities.detail: A beautifully preserved hilltop castle with stunning views over the town and Uwajima Bay, featuring original wooden structures and pleasant grounds to explore.
A scenic sandy beach on the coast near Uwajima offering swimming, beachside walks, and views of small islands dotting the Seto Inland Sea.
A museum dedicated to Uwajima's famous tradition of ushi toushin (bull sumo wrestling), with exhibits on the history and cultural significance of this rare local sport.
Witness live bull sumo wrestling competitions held during the July and November festivals, where bulls battle in a ring in a centuries-old tradition unique to Uwajima.
Explore the former mansion of the Date clan lords, offering insight into Edo-period samurai life and architecture with beautifully preserved rooms and gardens.
A historic Buddhist temple near the castle featuring traditional architecture and serene grounds that provide a peaceful retreat in the heart of town.

food.detail: Fresh local sea bream served raw and sliced thin, showcasing Uwajima's position as a premier fishing port with pristine daily catches.
A hearty local hot pot stew made with taro root, chicken, and dashi broth, traditionally prepared in autumn and winter as comfort food.
A regional noodle soup similar to Nagasaki champon, featuring thick wheat noodles in a rich seafood and vegetable broth unique to the Uwajima area.
Freshly boiled tiny whitebait served warm with soy sauce and citrus, a delicacy highlighting Uwajima's abundant coastal seafood.
Spicy marinated pollock roe, a local specialty that pairs well with rice and is available fresh at the morning fish market or local shops.

culture.detail: When attending bull sumo matches, respect the tradition by remaining quiet during fights and avoiding flash photography, as the bulls are sensitive to sudden disturbances.
Visit the local fish market early in the morning to see the day's catch and interact with fishmongers; this is when Uwajima's seafood culture is most vibrant.
Plan visits around July (Warei Festival) or November (Ushi Toushin Festival) to experience authentic local celebrations and bull wrestling in full cultural context.
Embrace the slower rhythm of life in Uwajima; allow extra time for meals, conversations with locals, and unhurried exploration of temples and coastal paths.

places.name: Asahi
places.description: A remote village in the Yodo Mountains known for gassho-style thatched farmhouses and traditional mountain village culture similar to Shirakawa-go. It offers an off-the-beaten-path experience of authentic Ehime mountain life.

activities.detail: Explore preserved thatched-roof farmhouses with steep triangular roofs designed to shed heavy mountain snow, some now operating as museums or homestays.
Trek through forested mountain trails surrounding the village with views of traditional terraced fields and pristine woodland typical of inland Shikoku.
Visit local artisans practicing traditional indigo dyeing and silk textile production, a heritage craft of Asahi's mountain communities.
Stroll through narrow lanes lined with stone walls, wooden bridges, and traditional irrigation channels (zenbei) that have shaped the village landscape for centuries.
Experience traditional fly-fishing or cormorant fishing techniques in seasonal mountain streams abundant with local river fish.
Discover small wooden shrines and temples nestled in the hills that reflect the spiritual traditions of isolated mountain settlements.

food.detail: Seasonal mountain vegetables and wild edible plants foraged from surrounding forests, lightly battered and fried, representing true mountain vegetable cuisine.
Freshwater trout from local streams grilled whole over charcoal, a signature protein of Asahi's pristine river valleys.
A traditional Buddhist vegetable broth with mountain root vegetables, tofu, and local herbs simmered in a light dashi, commonly served at farmhouse homestays.
Local mountain beef slowly braised in dark miso paste with regional vegetables, reflecting the village's cattle-raising heritage.
Harvest-season rice bowls topped with roasted chestnuts and millet gathered from the surrounding Yodo Mountains.

culture.detail: Many gassho homes are still active residences; ask permission before photographing and visit only designated public or homestay properties.
Asahi has minimal ATM access and few businesses accept cards, so withdraw sufficient yen before arriving in this remote mountain village.
Winter mountain snow can make access difficult; confirm road status with local tourism offices and plan visits during late spring through early autumn for reliable access.
Join in seasonal agricultural events like rice planting or harvest festivals if staying longer, as community participation is valued over passive observation.

restaurants.name: Yonemura
restaurants.description: Refined multi-course kaiseki showcasing seasonal Seto Inland Sea ingredients with meticulous presentation.

restaurants.name: Iya no Kazahashi
restaurants.description: Rustic countryside restaurant serving hearty local specialties including soba and mountain vegetables.

restaurants.name: Warakizushi
restaurants.description: Contemporary sushi counter featuring premium fish sourced from local fisheries with expert craftsmanship.

restaurants.name: Hiranoya
restaurants.description: Lively yakitori specialist grilling skewers over binchotan charcoal in a convivial standing atmosphere.

restaurants.name: Kyoto Sushidokoro Kiji
restaurants.description: Edomae-style sushi restaurant with 30+ years tradition, sourcing premium fish daily from regional markets.

restaurants.name: Toridon
restaurants.description: Simple standing counter serving piping-hot tori soboro donburi with tender chicken mince over rice.

restaurants.name: Sushidokoro Hamatomi
restaurants.description: Intimate eight-seat counter featuring ultra-fresh local catch including rare deep-sea species.

restaurants.name: Soba no Hana
restaurants.description: Rustic soba noodle shop in mountainous terrain renowned for hand-cut noodles and dashi broth.

restaurants.name: Mitarashi-tei
restaurants.description: Traditional okonomiyaki restaurant with griddle-side seating, layering ingredients with theatrical precision.

restaurants.name: Ushidori Hachiya
restaurants.description: Riverside specialist searing thick-cut Shimanto beef tataki over open flame, served with citrus and condiments.

restaurants.name: Kawakami
restaurants.description: Intimate farm-to-table restaurant highlighting mountain vegetables and seasonal ingredients from surrounding areas.

restaurants.name: Yamamotoya
restaurants.description: Historic restaurant specializing in imotaki, a rustic hot-pot of taro root and chicken simmered in rich broth.

restaurants.name: Arashiyama
restaurants.description: Classic Kagawa udon shop serving silky soft noodles in light dashi, a regional signature style.

restaurants.name: Hiiragi
restaurants.description: Coastal fine-dining venue showcasing Anan's renowned fresh seafood with elegant presentation and technique.

restaurants.name: Sobakinosato Inya
restaurants.description: Atmospheric soba restaurant in traditional thatched farmhouse serving buckwheat noodles with mountain herbs.
